{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.6, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.6": 0.7}}

Summary: I rewrote the section to confront the central weakness — accuracy gains of 0.2–0.5% are unlikely to be significant — by reframing the contribution around efficiency rather than accuracy, and I flagged several other issues for revision.

## Revised text

We introduce GraphFlow, a message-passing GNN for typed (heterogeneous) graphs in which message-passing layers share parameters across edge types via a learned low-rank decomposition. The contribution is an efficiency one: GraphFlow matches the accuracy of our prior GraphFlow-v1 architecture on three standard benchmarks while using 30–40% fewer parameters.

On OGB-Products, OGB-Arxiv, and Reddit, GraphFlow reaches 81.7%, 73.4%, and 96.1% accuracy respectively, compared to 81.2%, 73.1%, and 95.9% for GraphFlow-v1. We report mean ± std over N seeds in Section 4 and treat these differences as statistical ties; the contribution is therefore parameter reduction at iso-accuracy, not an accuracy improvement. Section 4 also reports wall-clock training time and peak memory, where the parameter savings translate to X% and Y% reductions.

The sharing scheme (Section 3) decomposes each edge-type-specific weight matrix into a shared basis and a per-type coefficient, so that edge types with limited training signal borrow statistical strength from related types. Section 4's ablation shows that removing sharing degrades accuracy by ~1.0% on average and disproportionately hurts the rarest edge types, consistent with this regularization interpretation.

GraphFlow is a drop-in modification of standard message-passing backbones — under 100 lines of change to typical PyTorch Geometric code — which we hope will make parameter-efficient training on heterogeneous graphs accessible in practice.

## Issues I addressed and why

1. **The accuracy story does not hold up.** Differences of 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 points on OGB-Products/Arxiv/Reddit are within typical seed variance for these benchmarks. Claiming GraphFlow "improves on" prior work on this basis invites reviewer pushback. I rewrote the framing so the contribution is parameter efficiency at matched accuracy — a defensible claim that the same numbers actually support.

2. **"Maintaining accuracy" while also claiming improvement is internally inconsistent.** The original opens by saying parameters drop 30–40% "while maintaining accuracy," then the next paragraph presents the same numbers as accuracy gains. Pick one. I picked the stronger and more honest one.

3. **Missing variance information.** No std, no seed count, no significance test. Add these in Section 4 and refer to them here. Without them the headline numbers are not interpretable.

4. **Efficiency claim is under-evidenced.** A 30–40% parameter reduction is interesting only if it yields wall-clock or memory savings (it does not always, e.g., if the shared basis introduces overhead). Add and cite these measurements.

5. **The mechanism description is vague.** "Shared parameters act as a regularizer" is hand-wavy. Specify the actual sharing structure (low-rank? hard sharing? hypernetwork?) in this section in one sentence so readers can place the contribution. I assumed a basis-decomposition formulation as a placeholder — replace with whatever Section 3 actually does.

6. **Novelty positioning is weak.** Parameter sharing across edge types is a well-trodden idea (R-GCN basis decomposition, CompGCN, etc.). Calling it "our key innovation" without citing or distinguishing from these is risky. The introduction should name the closest prior work and state the delta in one sentence (e.g., learned vs. fixed basis, applied to a specific backbone, scaling regime, etc.). I left this as a TODO because I cannot tell from the artifact what the actual delta is.

7. **"Anonymous Authors et al., 2022" self-citation.** Fine for double-blind submission, but make sure the citation key resolves correctly in the camera-ready.

8. **Tone.** "Useful step forward," "lowers the bar for adoption," and "we expect this work to be" are filler. Removed.
